title: Electronic Spectroscopy of Atomic Defects in Molybdenum Disulfide under Ambient Conditions
authors: Joshua R. Evans, Diego A. Garibay, Aiden N. Kuhls, Mehmet Z. Baykara
abstract: Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) attract significant attention as potential building blocks in next-generation electronic devices. On the other hand, a comprehensive understanding of how various defects affect local electronic properties under realistic operational conditions is yet to be formed. Here, we present results of electronic spectroscopy experiments performed on individual defects in the prototypical TMD molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) under ambient conditions, by way of conductive atomic force microscopy (C-AFM). Data acquired in the form of consecutive, high-resolution current maps at various bias voltages allow the assessment of local conductivity and differential conductance as a function of bias voltage for individual defects, the effects of which range from single atomic sites to several nanometers in lateral size. Characteristic behavior in spectroscopy data allows the categorization of observed defects into distinct groups, and their chemical identification as n-type and p-type transition metal substitutions of molybdenum atoms, as well as oxygen substitutions of sulfur atoms.
summary: This paper presents a new method for performing electronic spectroscopy on individual atomic defects in MoS2 under ambient conditions using C-AFM. By using a 'discrete I-V spectroscopy' technique, the authors can identify the chemical nature of defects, such as distinguishing between n-type/p-type metal substitutions and oxygen substitutions. This approach avoids the need for ultra-high vacuum environments, making it highly relevant for studying 2D materials in practical device-operating conditions. The study provides a way to map the electronic density of states of defects with high spatial resolution.
main_problem: The difficulty of identifying the chemical identity and electronic nature of atomic-scale defects in MoS2 under realistic, ambient conditions without the need for ultra-high vacuum environments.
main_result: The researchers successfully categorized and chemically identified various defects (n-type/p-type metal substitutions and oxygen substitutions) using a new C-AFM spectroscopy method, specifically identifying point defects as oxygen substitutions rather than sulfur vacancies.
method: A new 'Discrete I-V spectroscopy' approach using Conductive Atomic Force Microscopy (C-AFM) under ambient conditions, involving high-speed scanning of current maps at discrete bias voltages and polynomial fitting to extract differential conductance.
model_or_system: The physical system is molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) thin films, specifically focusing on atomic-scale defects including transition metal substitutions (Re, V, Nb) and oxygen substitutions at sulfur sites.
key_observables: Local conductivity, differential conductance (dI/dV-V), current maps, and spatial morphology of electronic perturbations.
important_parameters: Ambient conditions (22-23 C, 20-50% humidity), scan sizes from 10x10 nm2 to 150x150 nm2, and defect densities ranging from 10^11 to 10^12 cm-2.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the electronic signatures (dI/dV-V) can be reliably compared to existing STM and DFT data to achieve chemical identification.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows atomic-resolution current maps of single-atom and larger defects; Fig 2 displays current maps at different bias polarities showing changes in spatial morphology; Fig 3 presents I-V curves and derived differential conductance spectra for identified defects; Fig 4 demonstrates the identification of oxygen substitutions via current attenuation and lack of in-gap states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of defect characterization, describes the new C-AFM discrete I-V spectroscopy methodology, presents the categorization of substitution defects, details the identification of oxygen-based point defects, and concludes with a comparison of findings to existing literature.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
